intro.title: One Protocol, Any Model, Any Tool
intro.sub: The standard that collapses bespoke integrations into one wire AI can actually use. Six acts, from the sprawl to your own server.

acts.title: The Universal Adapter for AI

lines: But what is it, and why was it even built?
To really get it, you have to feel the problem first.
So let's rewind to life without MCP, back when it was just models and agents.

acts.body: Inside the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and the structural shift in agent architecture.
acts.reducedMotionNote: Start with one model wired to 4 services: 4 bespoke integrations. Swap that model for one that speaks a different tool-calling format (Claude uses Anthropic's tool-use schema; OpenAI, DeepSeek and others use the function-calling schema) and all 4 break, because the schemas aren't interchangeable. Now multiply by every model your team runs: 3 models × 4 services = 12 hand-written integrations to build and maintain.

painPoints.title: Every service's API behaves differently
painPoints.note: Different auth, request/response schemas, pagination, error formats. One service can even expose several APIs (GitHub has REST and GraphQL), and no two are alike.

painPoints.title: Every integration is bespoke glue
painPoints.note: M models × N services = M×N hand-written integrations to build and maintain. Each ◆ on a wire is one custom connector.

painPoints.title: Swap the model and it breaks
painPoints.note: Tool-calling schemas are provider-specific. Anthropic tool-use, OpenAI functions and Google function-calling are all different. Tight coupling, zero portability.

acts.swapCaption: Switched Claude for DeepSeek. Claude speaks Anthropic's tool-use format; DeepSeek speaks the OpenAI-style function-calling format. Same services, but a different tool-calling schema, so the 4 Anthropic-shaped integrations break and have to be rebuilt. Zero portability.
acts.revealCaption: And you rarely run just one model. Every model your team uses needs its own glue to every service: 3 models × 4 services = 12 bespoke integrations to build and keep alive.

analogy.lead: Like a bag of travel plug adapters.
points: Every device needs its own plug for every country's socket
3 gadgets across 4 countries = 12 adapters in the bag
That tangle is the M×N problem

acts.title: The Standard
acts.body: One open standard that every app and service speaks, so the M×N tangle of bespoke glue collapses to a clean M+N.
acts.oldCaption: Writing glue code for every service. Teaching endpoints via massive system prompts.
acts.mcpCaption: Servers advertise capabilities. The model reasons about what to use and when. Zero glue code.
acts.httpAnalogy: Just as HTTP unified how browsers talk to servers without changing the databases underneath, MCP is unifying how models talk to tools. Expose your systems once and any compliant model can use them, with no custom integration per service.

points: One port replaced the drawer of chargers
Plug into any service, no rewrites

acts.origin: Introduced by Anthropic in November 2024, modeled on the Language Server Protocol that standardized IDE tooling.
acts.adoption: OpenAI, Google, and Microsoft all shipped support within a year, and in December 2025 it was donated to the Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation. By 2026 it's the de facto standard for agent tooling, with around 10,000 active MCP servers and over 90 million monthly SDK downloads.

acts.title: APIs Were Never Built for AI Models
acts.body: APIs were built for a human integrator. Watch how a person actually wires one up.
narration: A developer opens the API docs and starts reading.
They experiment, hit edge cases, and re-read. Weeks pass.
Finally they write the one correct call and freeze it as code.
That code invokes the API and just runs, a million times over.
acts.insight: The hard thinking happened up front, just once, and got cached as code.
acts.bridge: AI models can't cache calls like that. They generate each step probabilistically and reason through uncertain inputs, often asking, clarifying, or exploring before knowing what to do.

points: Weeks of testing happen once, then it's frozen on the card
A line cook reproduces it identically forever
An AI is the home cook, improvising and tasting each time

acts.title: Under the Hood
acts.body: MCP is a simple client-server model. Your host app runs a client; each service runs a server that exposes what the model can use.

primitives.name: tools
primitives.note: executable actions the model calls to do something, like create an issue or run a query

primitives.name: resources
primitives.note: read-only data the server exposes by URI for context, like files, schemas, or logs

primitives.name: prompts
primitives.note: reusable instruction templates the server ships, triggered by the user

narration: A host app runs an MCP client. Each service runs an MCP server.
The server exposes 3 primitives: **tools** to act, **resources** to read, and **prompts** to reuse.
On connect, the server advertises what it can do, so the client discovers its tools at runtime instead of hardcoding them.
The client calls a tool, the server runs it and returns the result. That loop repeats for every action.
acts.insight: The server advertises its capabilities, so the agent discovers and uses them at runtime. Nothing is hardcoded.

points: Dishes you can order = tools
An ingredients card you can read = resources
Set-menu combos = prompts
You read it each visit, never memorizing the kitchen

acts.title: The Handshake
acts.body: The MCP client and server talk over JSON-RPC 2.0. The client doesn't hardcode endpoints. It asks the server what it can do at runtime, then calls it.
acts.keyPoint: tools/list is dynamic discovery. That's the real difference from a hardcoded API.

points: Greet, agree on a language, ask what's on today
Then place your order
Every dish returns on your ticket number, so replies never get mixed up

acts.jsonrpcNote: JSON-RPC 2.0 is a tiny request/response format. The client sends a method name, params, and an id; the server replies with the same id and a result. Every MCP message is just one of these. Watch one full exchange, step by step:

transports.name: stdio
transports.note: local: client spawns the server as a subprocess

messages.label: initialize
messages.note: client offers its protocol version + capabilities

messages.label: initialize result
messages.note: server replies with its identity + capabilities

messages.label: tools/list
messages.note: DYNAMIC DISCOVERY: what can you do?

messages.label: tools/call
messages.note: model picks a tool and fills the arguments

messages.label: tools/call result
messages.note: server runs it and returns structured content

acts.title: MCP is an Abstraction, Not a Replacement
acts.body: APIs still exist and do the heavy lifting. MCP makes them model-friendly.
acts.callout: The model never sees your REST APIs. It only interacts through the structured MCP schema, handling discovery, validation, and execution uniformly.
acts.insight: Same APIs underneath. MCP just makes them speak the model's language.

analogy.lead: Like a translator at an embassy.
points: You speak your language
They handle the foreign forms and offices underneath
Those never change: the model talks to MCP, MCP talks to your APIs

acts.title: Wire It Yourself: API vs MCP
acts.body: Every provider needs different code. MCP writes it once. Pick a model, run it, then swap and watch the left side rewrite.

api.name: Weather API
api.desc: A normal REST endpoint. To call it from an AI model you must describe it in that provider's tool-calling format — or expose it once through MCP.

steps: Now just swap — MCP never asks
acts.apiColLabel: Without MCP — wire each provider yourself

openai.parseNote: arguments arrives as a JSON string — you must json.loads() it

anthropic.parseNote: input is already a Python dict on the tool_use block — no json.loads() needed

google.parseNote: functionCall.args is a dict nested under candidates[0].content.parts[0]

lines: # you write nothing — server owns this

narration.initial: ① Pick a model and click **Run** — see the live call land. Then swap providers and feel what changes.
narration.run: The call landed on both sides. Now **swap the model** and watch what the left side has to do next.
narration.pendingRewrite: **{model}** speaks a different schema ({schema}). The Direct-API integration breaks until you hand-rewrite it. **Approve the rewrite** to apply it yourself.
narration.approved: You just hand-rewrote **{count} lines** across all three stages. Imagine doing that for every model and every tool. MCP needed zero changes.
narration.swapAuto: Swapped to **{model}**. Left rewrote {count} lines again — you've felt the pain, now just watch it pile up. MCP: still zero.
narration.swapSame: **{model}** uses the same format as before. Nothing changed on either side.
narration.done: Keep swapping. The friction climbs every time. The MCP counter never moves.

acts.reducedMotionNote: Both windows show the same three stages: define the tool, make the call, parse the result. On the left (without MCP) all three stages change every time you switch provider — different schema, different SDK call, different parse path. On the right (with MCP) all three are frozen: the server advertises the tool, one call goes out, one result shape comes back. Swap providers on the left and the friction counter climbs; the MCP counter stays at zero.

analogy.lead: Like a bag of country-specific plug adapters vs one universal adapter.
points: Every device needs its own adapter for every country's socket
Swap a device and you're back to hunting for the right plug
A universal adapter works everywhere, no matter what you plug in
